scenario: A team migrating from older Spring Zeebe versions sees @ZeebeWorker annotations in legacy code, and newer code uses @JobWorker. They wonder about the difference. What's the difference between @ZeebeWorker and @JobWorker in Spring Zeebe?
question: What's the difference between @ZeebeWorker and @JobWorker in Spring Zeebe?

options.text: @ZeebeWorker and @JobWorker are identical aliases with no meaningful difference -- this conflates a legacy annotation with its modern replacement. While both annotations serve the same functional purpose of registering a method as a job handler, @ZeebeWorker is the older annotation from the legacy Spring Zeebe SDK, and @JobWorker is the current canonical annotation from the Camunda Spring Boot Starter. Using @ZeebeWorker in new code introduces a deprecated dependency that may be removed in future versions.

8 per Pattern #18; spring-zeebe-sdk artifact was renamed/superseded by camunda-spring-boot-starter). Functionally similar -- both mark a method as a job handler subscribed to a task type. ). JobWorker).

@ZeebeWorker is designed specifically for gRPC transport while @JobWorker works over REST -- this invents a transport-based distinction with no basis in the documented API. Both annotations work with the same underlying Zeebe client transport and handle the same job activation, processing, and completion lifecycle. The difference is purely about annotation origin and naming: @ZeebeWorker comes from the legacy library and @JobWorker from the modern Camunda Spring Boot Starter.

@ZeebeWorker provides additional features that @JobWorker lacks -- this inverts the evolution direction. @JobWorker is the modern replacement with a cleaner API aligned with the renamed Camunda Spring Boot Starter artifact. If any attribute differences exist between the two annotations, they reflect API improvements in the newer @JobWorker version rather than missing features. For new projects, @JobWorker is preferred and @ZeebeWorker is the legacy variant to migrate away from.

a.text: Incorrect. Spring Zeebe evolved its annotation naming: @ZeebeWorker (legacy): the original annotation in older Spring Zeebe versions. @JobWorker (modern): renamed in later versions; aligned with the abstract "job" concept (a job in Zeebe is what a worker handles). Migration: Replace @ZeebeWorker with @JobWorker. Attributes (type, retries, autoComplete, etc.) carried over; verify per migration docs for any renamed parameters. The legacy annotation may be deprecated or removed in newer Spring Zeebe versions. For new code: always use @JobWorker. For legacy code being migrated: update the annotation as part of the migration. Practical scope (per docs evolution): Both subscribe to a Zeebe task type and handle activated jobs. Both support similar configuration (type, timeout, autoComplete, fetchVariables, etc.). Implementation details differ slightly between Spring Zeebe versions; verify per version.
